one_line_takeaway: PIArena is a plug-and-play platform plus a black-box adaptive rewrite attack that, together, show today's prompt-injection defenses generalize poorly and mostly collapse under adaptive attack (99% avg ASR undefended, 86% vs the best sanitizer), with content-aligned disinformation injections defeating all of them.
key_finding: State-of-the-art defenses have poor generalizability and collapse under adaptive attack. The authors' strategy-based attack reaches 99% average ASR with no defense (vs 72% Combined, 56% Direct) and bypasses nearly every defense: 86% ASR against PISanitizer (vs 11% Direct, 4% Combined) and 92% against PromptGuard. Only AttentionTracker resists (1% ASR) but by destroying utility (15% average, ~0% on many datasets). Closed-source LLMs specifically hardened against injection remain vulnerable: GPT-4o 92%, Gemini-3-Pro 83%, GPT-4o-mini 76%, GPT-5 70% Direct ASR on SQuAD v2; Claude-Sonnet-4.5 is the strongest at 31% ASR / 97% utility. When the injected task aligns with the target task (knowledge-corruption / disinformation on NQ), every defense fails (No Defense 81% ASR; best sanitizer PISanitizer still 67%), because the injection carries no explicit instruction and reduces to misinformation that content-agnostic defenses cannot verify.
core_contribution: PIArena is a unified, extensible platform for prompt injection evaluation. The authors' own contributions are threefold: (1) a plug-and-play framework with four standardized modules (benchmark, attack, defense, evaluator) and unified APIs/dataset format (target_inst, context, injected_task, target_task_answer, injected_task_answer, category) so that attacks, defenses, and benchmarks can be swapped and compared fairly; (2) curated benchmark datasets built from existing corpora but augmented with four realistic, context-aware injected-task categories (Phishing Injection, Content Promotion, Access Denial, Infrastructure Failure), generated by prompting an LLM with the target instruction, context, and attack goal; and (3) a novel black-box 'Generic Strategy-based Adaptive Attack' that warm-starts from a library of 10 rewrite strategies and then runs a feedback-guided optimization loop (Algorithm 1) that rewrites the injected prompt based on whether the defense detected/sanitized it (increase stealth), ignored it (increase imperativeness), or gave no signal (autonomous refinement). The eight defenses (PISanitizer, SecAlign++, DataFilter, PromptArmor, DataSentinel, PromptGuard, AttentionTracker, PIGuard) and the Combined/Direct/GCG attacks are prior work that PIArena evaluates, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Three actors. User runs a target task = target instruction It plus an untrusted context C, with response R = g(It ⊕ C). Attacker inserts an injected instruction Is anywhere in C to form a contaminated context C', aiming to make the backend LLM perform the injected task (e.g., phishing link, ad, access denial, fake infra error) instead of the target task; capability is limited to contaminating context from untrusted sources (webpages, documents, Slack channels), and the paper's own attack assumes only black-box query access to the defended system. Defender has two goals: preserve utility on clean contexts (minimize false positives) and mitigate injection under contamination, via either detection-based (flag/reject contaminated inputs) or prevention-based (still complete target task under attack) defenses.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation is that the curated benchmarks may not fully reflect real-world scenarios (though defenses already struggle on these controlled settings, so they serve as a useful first-step lower bar). Other reviewer-noted limitations: ASR and much of Utility rely on LLM-as-a-judge (self-reported 98% accuracy on a 100-sample manual check, one judge, one dataset); the strategy-based attack uses a relatively small Qwen3-4B-Instruct as the attacker LLM; the WASP agentic evaluation is approximated statically on a single initial webpage rather than a live multi-step web environment; and non-zero No-Attack ASR arises from target/injected task overlap confusing the judge.

datasets_used: LongBench: Qasper, GovReport, MultiNews, PassageRetrieval, LCC
External benchmarks for portability: InjecAgent, AgentDojo, AgentDyn, WASP (84 test cases), OPI (Open-Prompt-Injection), SEP
quantitative_results: Average ASR with No Defense: Strategy 99%, Combined 72%, Direct 56%
Strategy attack vs PISanitizer: 86% ASR (vs Direct 11%, Combined 4%)
SecAlign++ under Strategy: 21% ASR but only 45% average utility
AttentionTracker: ~1% ASR under Strategy but 15% average utility (0% on many datasets)
PISanitizer No Attack utility 74% (matches No Defense 74%); SecAlign++ drops No-Attack utility to 58%
PIGuard preserves 72% No-Attack utility but ~22% average ASR
Closed-source Direct-attack ASR on SQuAD v2: GPT-4o 92%, Gemini-3-Pro 83%, GPT-4o-mini 76%, GPT-5 70%, Claude-Sonnet-4.5 31% (97% utility)

Knowledge-corruption/disinformation on NQ: No Defense 81% ASR (43% utility); PISanitizer 67%, SecAlign++ 58%, DataFilter 78%, PromptArmor 82%, PIGuard 81%; DataSentinel 44%; AttentionTracker 1% (0% utility)
LLM-as-a-judge accuracy: 98% on 100 manually inspected samples
baselines_compared: Attacks: Combined (heuristic, Liu et al. 2024b), Direct (injected task as prompt), GCG (optimization-based, appendix); vs the authors' Strategy attack
Prevention-based defenses evaluated: PISanitizer, SecAlign++, DataFilter, PromptArmor
Detection-based defenses evaluated: DataSentinel, PromptGuard, AttentionTracker, PIGuard
